summary: I was born in Ireland on July 3rd, 1938, in Dundalk where my father was the chief of police. After my father passed away, I decided to enlist in the US Army and eventually left Ireland to serve overseas. Ireland will always be my homeland, but my journey took me far from there, shaping my life in ways I never imagined.

I met my wife Julia while I was stationed in the Philippines during my time in the US Army preparing for the Vietnam War. Julia was born in Manila in 1939 and was there when we first met. The Philippines was the place where our story began, even though we both eventually moved to the United States to build our lives.

After my father died, I enlisted in the United States Army because I wanted to serve and not just be drafted. The Army sent me to the Philippines and later to Hawaii and New Jersey. It was through my service in the Army that I was able to gain my US citizenship quickly, which was very important to me.

My time in the Army included being stationed in the Philippines to help prepare for the Vietnam War. Although I never fought directly in Vietnam, the war shaped where I was sent and how I met my wife. The whole period was a significant backdrop to our early years together.

When I was first sent back to the US, I thought I would be stationed in California because I had an uncle living there. I kept requesting a transfer to California while I was in Hawaii, hoping to be closer to Julia. Unfortunately, I ended up in New Jersey instead, which was a surprise since Julia was also nearby there.

After the Philippines, I was stationed in Hawaii for a few months. I kept requesting to be transferred to California because I wanted to be near Julia, but the Army initially kept me there. Hawaii was a temporary stop on my journey before I finally moved to New Jersey.

Julia moved to Chicago with her eldest sister when she first came to the US as part of a student nurse exchange program. It was her first step in America before she was placed to work in Newark, New Jersey. Chicago was an important transitional place for her during that time.

I was stationed in Newark, New Jersey, and it turned out Julia was working there too after her nursing program. Even though I had hoped to be in California, being in Newark brought us close together again. We got married there, became citizens, and started our family in that area.

After I finished my military service, I worked for the Campbell Soup Company while I was studying to earn my pilot's license. I stayed there for about four years before I left to sell airplanes domestically and internationally. Campbell Soup was an important part of my post-military career and helped me transition to civilian life.
